title: DIG DHT RPC — content-discovery wire (dig-dht)
description: The Kademlia DHT RPC that locates WHICH peers hold a piece of content. This is NOT JSON-RPC 2.0 (the dig.* methods in openrpc.json / openrpc-node.json are): it is a distinct, type-tagged JSON wire carried as a u32 big-endian length-prefixed body over an authenticated dig-nat mTLS stream (byte-identical framing to the relay RelayMessage control messages). A node runs find_providers to get the peer_ids + candidate addresses of holders, then reaches them over the NAT ladder and fetches with dig.getAvailability + dig.fetchRange. dig-nat/dig-dht/dig-node conform to this frozen contract.

transport.over: authenticated dig-nat mTLS stream (peer_id = SHA-256(TLS SubjectPublicKeyInfo DER))
transport.framing: u32 big-endian body-length prefix, then a type-tagged JSON body (same framing as the relay RelayMessage control messages)
transport.callerIdentity: the responder folds the mTLS-verified caller (its Contact) into its routing table on every inbound RPC; the caller identity comes from the authenticated transport, never a request field

keyspace.description: One 256-bit XOR (Kademlia) metric for both nodes and content.
keyspace.nodeId: a node's key IS its peer_id (already a uniform 256-bit SHA-256 value)
keyspace.distance: XOR of the two 32-byte keys, compared big-endian (smaller = closer)
keyspace.bucketIndex: 255 - leading_zeros(distance) — the position of the most-significant set bit (256 k-buckets)

contentKey.derivation: SHA-256(domain_tag ‖ canonical_bytes) — one leading domain-tag byte then the raw 32-byte fields in fixed order; the tag keeps the three granularities distinct points

granularities.answers: does a peer serve this store?
does a peer have this generation store_id:root (the immutable capsule)?
does a peer have this resource within the capsule?

methods.name: find_node
methods.summary: Ask for the k peers the responder knows closest to an XOR-metric target key.

methods.name: find_providers
methods.summary: Ask for providers of a content key. Returns any provider records the responder holds PLUS (always) the k closer contacts, so an iterative lookup keeps converging toward the key even when providers are already found.

methods.name: add_provider
methods.summary: Announce that record.provider_peer_id holds record.content_key. Sent to the k nodes closest to the content key; the responder stores the record (soft state, TTL'd).

methods.name: ping
methods.summary: Liveness check; the responder echoes the nonce in its pong.

errorEnvelope.semantics: advisory — a lookup treats an error response like an unreachable peer and walks on to the next contact

Contact.description: A peer's identity plus how to reach it. addresses[] is byte-compatible with the L7 dig.getPeers addresses shape, so a Contact drops straight into a dial target for the NAT ladder.

ProviderRecord.description: The DHT's stored value: peer provider_peer_id holds the content whose key is content_key, reachable at addresses, until expires_at. Soft state — TTL'd, republished before expiry, and GC'd when stale.

fields.expires_at: <uint> — absolute Unix seconds; a record at or after this time is stale/absent

CandidateAddr.description: One candidate address, byte-compatible with the L7 dig.getPeers addresses[] shape. The dialer prefers most-direct-first (direct < mapped < reflexive < relay).

fields.kind: one of: direct | mapped | reflexive | relay

providerRecordLifecycle.announce: on gaining content it serves, a node add_providers a record binding the content key to its own peer_id + candidate addresses, at the k nodes closest to the key
providerRecordLifecycle.republish: the holder re-announces (fresh expires_at) on an interval strictly shorter than the TTL, so records never expire while it is online
providerRecordLifecycle.withdraw: on losing content, the node stops announcing it; the record ages out on its TTL (no explicit delete)
providerRecordLifecycle.gc: a responder discards expired records on read and never returns them
